Can you explain how closures work in JavaScript?

A closure is a function that has access to variables from its outer (enclosing) scope, even after the outer function has returned.

Here's a simple example:

function createCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function() {
    count++;
    return count;
  };
}

const counter = createCounter();
console.log(counter()); // 1
console.log(counter()); // 2

The inner function closes over the count variable.

What are some practical use cases?

Here are some common use cases for closures:

  • Data privacy — creating private variables
  • Factory functions — generating specialized functions
  • Event handlers — preserving state in callbacks
